question: If a catch event exists identically in the source and the target process but no mapping instruction is provided between the two catch events, what happens during migration?

options.text: The existing subscription is closed and a new identical subscription is opened on the target catch event, effectively resetting it.
The subscription is automatically migrated because the source and target catch events are identical.
The catch event is removed from the migrated instance because it lacks an explicit mapping.
An incident is raised; the engine requires an explicit mapping when source and target catch events match.

a.text: When the mapping instruction between catch events is omitted, the source subscription is closed and a new one is opened on the target. Even when the events are identical, this resets the subscription state — for example, restarting a timer from zero.

b.text: Subscriptions are migrated only when an explicit mapping instruction is provided between source and target catch events, regardless of how similar they look.

c.text: Catch events are not removed from the migrated instance because they lack a mapping; a new subscription is opened on the target catch event.

d.text: Mapping catch events is optional; omitting it is the documented way to reset the subscription, not an error.

explanation: Mapping instructions on catch events are optional. When omitted, the source subscription is closed and a new subscription is opened on the target catch event. Even when source and target are identical, this resets the subscription state — for instance, a timer restarts from zero rather than preserving its elapsed time.
